title: Goodbye, Banker Life
description: Monsters are approaching the city, and to protect it, Akito must create a protective field around the city. As everyone knows, protective fields come in various levels. Akito has chosen the field of level $$$n$$$. To construct the field, a special phrase is required, which is the $$$n$$$-th row of the Great Magical Triangle, represented as a two-dimensional array. We will call this array $$$T$$$. The triangle is defined as follows: In the $$$i$$$-th row, there are $$$i$$$ integers. The single integer in the first row is $$$k$$$. Let the $$$j$$$-th element of the $$$i$$$-th row be denoted as $$$T_{i,j}$$$. Then $$$$$$T_{i,j} = \begin{cases} T_{i-1,j-1} \oplus T_{i-1,j}, &\textrm{if } 1  \lt  j  \lt  i \\ T_{i-1,j}, &\textrm{if } j = 1 \\ T_{i-1,j-1}, &\textrm{if } j = i \end{cases}$$$$$$ where $$$a \oplus b$$$ is the bitwise exclusive "OR" ( XOR ) of the integers $$$a$$$ and $$$b$$$. Help Akito find the integers in the $$$n$$$-th row of the infinite triangle before the monsters reach the city.
input_spec: The first line contains the integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$) — the number of test cases. In the only line of each test case, there are two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$k$$$ ($$$1 \le n \le 10^6,\ 1 \le k  \lt  2^{31}$$$) — the row index that Akito needs and the integer in the first row of the Great Magical Triangle, respectively. It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ across all test cases does not exceed $$$10^6$$$.
output_spec: For each test case, output $$$n$$$ integers — the elements of the $$$n$$$-th row of the Great Magical Triangle.

routes.name: XOR Pascal's triangle via Lucas' theorem
routes.description: Recognize the triangle as Pascal's triangle under XOR (mod-2 arithmetic), so each entry is either k or 0 depending on whether the corresponding binomial coefficient is odd. Use Kummer's theorem (equivalently Lucas' theorem) to test parity of C(n-1, j-1) efficiently via the 2-adic valuation of factorials.

observations.title: XOR behaves like addition modulo 2 on each bit independently
observations.description: Since XOR operates on each bit position independently and is equivalent to addition mod 2 within each bit, the entire triangle decomposes into independent single-bit problems. For any fixed bit position, the triangle on that bit is exactly Pascal's triangle modulo 2. This means each entry T_{i,j} depends only on how many times k appears in the sum C(i-1, j-1) * k under mod-2 arithmetic: T_{i,j} = k if C(i-1, j-1) is odd, and T_{i,j} = 0 if C(i-1, j-1) is even.

hints.nudge: XOR is addition without carries. What well-known triangle uses addition to combine entries from the row above?
hints.question: If you replace + with XOR in Pascal's triangle recurrence and start with k instead of 1, what is the general formula for the entry at row i, position j?
hints.formal: XOR is addition in GF(2) applied bitwise. So T_{i,j} = C(i-1, j-1) * k in GF(2)^{31}, meaning T_{i,j} = k when C(i-1, j-1) is odd, and T_{i,j} = 0 when C(i-1, j-1) is even.

observations.title: Verify with small examples: the XOR triangle matches Pascal's triangle mod 2
observations.description: Row 3 with k=16: Pascal's row 2 is [1, 2, 1], and mod 2 that is [1, 0, 1], giving [16, 0, 16]. Row 9 with k=1: Pascal's row 8 is [1,8,28,56,70,56,28,8,1], and mod 2 only the endpoints are odd, giving [1,0,0,0,0,0,0,0,1]. These match the sample outputs exactly, confirming the connection to binomial coefficient parity.

hints.nudge: Try computing the first few rows by hand with k=1. Does the pattern of 0s and non-zeros remind you of a famous fractal?
hints.question: Compute row 3 (i=3) by hand using the XOR recurrence. Compare C(2,0), C(2,1), C(2,2) modulo 2. Do they match?
hints.formal: Row 3 of XOR triangle: T_{3,1} = T_{2,1} = k, T_{3,2} = T_{2,1} XOR T_{2,2} = k XOR k = 0, T_{3,3} = T_{2,2} = k. C(2,0)=1 (odd), C(2,1)=2 (even), C(2,2)=1 (odd). Pattern matches: k, 0, k.

observations.title: C(n, j) is odd iff there are no carries when adding j and n-j in binary (Kummer's theorem)
observations.description: By Kummer's theorem, the highest power of prime p dividing C(n, j) equals the number of carries when adding j and n-j in base p. For p=2, C(n, j) is odd if and only if there are zero carries, which happens if and only if every bit of j is <= the corresponding bit of n. Equivalently, C(n, j) is odd iff (j AND n) == j, i.e., j is a submask of n in binary.

hints.nudge: There is a classical theorem relating the prime factorization of binomial coefficients to carries in base-p addition. What is it called?
hints.question: Kummer's theorem says v_p(C(a+b, a)) equals the number of carries in base-p addition of a and b. For p=2, when are there zero carries?
hints.formal: By Kummer's theorem, v_2(C(n, j)) = number of carries when adding j and (n-j) in binary. Zero carries occur iff for every bit position, bit_j + bit_{n-j} <= 1, which is equivalent to j being a submask of n: (j & n) == j.

observations.title: Reformulate using 2-adic valuation of factorials for efficient precomputation
observations.description: Define v(m) = the 2-adic valuation of m! (the total number of times 2 divides m!). By Legendre's formula, v(m) = sum_{i>=1} floor(m / 2^i). Then C(n, j) is odd iff v(n) = v(j) + v(n-j), because v(n) - v(j) - v(n-j) counts the carries. Precompute v(m) for all m from 0 to max(n)-1 using a simple recurrence: v(0) = 0, v(m) = v(m-1) + (number of times 2 divides m). This runs in O(n) time.

hints.nudge: You need to check the parity condition for each j in row n. Can you precompute some function of factorials to make each check O(1)?
hints.question: If v(m) is the exponent of 2 in m!, what is the relationship between v(n), v(j), and v(n-j) when C(n, j) is odd?
hints.formal: v_2(C(n, j)) = v_2(n!) - v_2(j!) - v_2((n-j)!). So C(n, j) is odd iff v(n) == v(j) + v(n-j). Precompute v incrementally: v(0) = 0; for m >= 1, v(m) = v(m-1) + ctz(m), where ctz(m) counts trailing zeros of m in binary (equivalently, the 2-adic valuation of m itself).

observations.title: Output k or 0 per position using the precomputed valuation array
observations.description: For each test case with parameters n and k, iterate j from 0 to n-1. Output k if v(n-1) == v(j) + v(n-1-j), and 0 otherwise. The precomputation of v is done once across all test cases. Since the sum of n is at most 10^6 and each position requires O(1) work, the total time is O(sum of n), which fits within the time limit.

hints.nudge: You have reduced the problem to a single equality check per position. What do you output for each position?
hints.question: Given the precomputed array v, what is the complete loop body for outputting row n?
hints.formal: Precompute c[0..MAXN] where c[0]=0 and c[m] = c[m-1] + v_2(m). For each test case (n, k): let r = n-1. For j = 0 to r: print k if c[r] == c[j] + c[r-j], else print 0. Total complexity: O(MAXN) precomputation + O(sum of n) per test.
